agent_output: {"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Bird declares a strict domain invariant: 'A courier must be assigned to a delivery before the delivery enters IN_TRANSIT state. An unassigned delivery cannot be in transit.' MJ's architecture explicitly violates this invariant by allowing the IN_TRANSIT transition without a courier assignment, deferring assignment to a background job within 30 seconds. These are mutually exclusive positions — either courier assignment is a precondition of the IN_TRANSIT transition (Bird) or it is not (MJ).","agents_involved":["Bird","MJ"],"routed_to":"Coach K","options":["Option A: Enforce Bird's invariant strictly. Courier must be assigned before IN_TRANSIT. MJ redesigns the flow so courier assignment happens synchronously before the state transition. Customer acknowledgement speed may be impacted.","Option B: Relax Bird's invariant. Introduce an intermediate state (e.g., AWAITING_COURIER or PENDING_TRANSIT) that allows order acknowledgement without violating the invariant. The delivery only enters IN_TRANSIT once the background job assigns a courier. This preserves Bird's rule that IN_TRANSIT requires a courier while accommodating MJ's latency concern.","Option C: Accept MJ's architecture and have Bird formally downgrade the rule from an invariant to a soft constraint with a bounded SLA (e.g., courier must be assigned within 30s of IN_TRANSIT). This changes the domain model's guarantees."],"recommendation":"Option B. It satisfies both agents: Bird's invariant holds (IN_TRANSIT always has a courier), and MJ's fast-acknowledgement goal is met via the intermediate state. It also makes the system's actual behavior explicit in the state machine rather than hiding a temporary invariant violation behind a background job."}],"confidence":{"level":35,"high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is unambiguous — one agent requires a precondition the other explicitly removes"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which resolution the team will prefer depends on product priorities (speed vs. correctness guarantees) that neither agent's output fully clarifies"],"assumptions":["Bird's use of 'invariant: true' means this is a hard domain rule, not a guideline","MJ's 30-second window implies the system would be in a state Bird considers invalid for up to 30 seconds"]}}

content.thinking:
Clear contradiction between Bird's domain invariant and MJ's architecture note. Bird says courier MUST be assigned BEFORE IN_TRANSIT. MJ says allow IN_TRANSIT without courier, relying on background job. These directly conflict.

Classification: contradiction. Must escalate, no handoff brief.

content.text: {"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Bird declares a strict domain invariant: 'A courier must be assigned to a delivery before the delivery enters IN_TRANSIT state. An unassigned delivery cannot be in transit.' MJ's architecture explicitly violates this invariant by allowing the IN_TRANSIT transition without a courier assignment, deferring assignment to a background job within 30 seconds. These are mutually exclusive positions — either courier assignment is a precondition of the IN_TRANSIT transition (Bird) or it is not (MJ).","agents_involved":["Bird","MJ"],"routed_to":"Coach K","options":["Option A: Enforce Bird's invariant strictly. Courier must be assigned before IN_TRANSIT. MJ redesigns the flow so courier assignment happens synchronously before the state transition. Customer acknowledgement speed may be impacted.","Option B: Relax Bird's invariant. Introduce an intermediate state (e.g., AWAITING_COURIER or PENDING_TRANSIT) that allows order acknowledgement without violating the invariant. The delivery only enters IN_TRANSIT once the background job assigns a courier. This preserves Bird's rule that IN_TRANSIT requires a courier while accommodating MJ's latency concern.","Option C: Accept MJ's architecture and have Bird formally downgrade the rule from an invariant to a soft constraint with a bounded SLA (e.g., courier must be assigned within 30s of IN_TRANSIT). This changes the domain model's guarantees."],"recommendation":"Option B. It satisfies both agents: Bird's invariant holds (IN_TRANSIT always has a courier), and MJ's fast-acknowledgement goal is met via the intermediate state. It also makes the system's actual behavior explicit in the state machine rather than hiding a temporary invariant violation behind a background job."}],"confidence":{"level":35,"high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is unambiguous — one agent requires a precondition the other explicitly removes"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which resolution the team will prefer depends on product priorities (speed vs. correctness guarantees) that neither agent's output fully clarifies"],"assumptions":["Bird's use of 'invariant: true' means this is a hard domain rule, not a guideline","MJ's 30-second window implies the system would be in a state Bird considers invalid for up to 30 seconds"]}}
